dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to EMS for a medical alarm, elderly female on the floor with altered mental status.
Hi ma'am, we're with EMS. We're here to help you.
She is hard of hearing and was found on the floor.
We're told you may have had a low blood sugar episode and seem confused right now.
I don't think I need anything. I'm okay.
She was found on the floor and has been slow to answer questions.
Her eyes are open spontaneously.
She is answering slowly and appears confused.
She localizes and moves appropriately when stimulated.
Initial responsiveness is altered, approximately GCS 13.
Airway is patent and self-maintained, though saliva is noted drooping from the mouth.
Breathing is spontaneous, about 16 to 20 per minute, adequate in depth, and non-labored.
Radial pulse is strong and regular. No major bleeding is seen.
Skin is cool, moist, and normal in color.
Patient is an elderly female found lying supine on the floor against the wall under a window. A blood smear is present on the wall and a skin tear is noted on the right elbow.
Blood glucose is 48 milligrams per deciliter.
Blood pressure is 100 over 62.
Pulse is 58.
Respiratory rate is 20.
Four-lead monitor shows sinus rhythm with occasional PVCs at about 50 to 70 beats per minute.
When was the last time you had anything to eat or drink?
I don't remember exactly, but sometime around 6 or 7 last night.
Do you remember what happened before you ended up on the floor?
I think I may have had a low sugar spell, then I fell.
Right elbow skin tear is cleaned with gauze and normal saline, then dressed with gauze and cling.
A 20-gauge IV is established in the right hand and secured with a saline lock.
Administering approximately 125 milliliters of D10 intravenously for hypoglycemia after medication cross-check.
Mental status is rapidly improving after D10 administration.
Repeat blood glucose is 253 milligrams per deciliter, so the D10 infusion is stopped.
Patient is now alert and oriented times four with GCS improved to 15.
Ma'am, your blood sugar was low. You've responded well to treatment and seem much more alert now.
Did you take your insulin tonight?
Yes, I took my insulin around 9 PM without checking my blood sugar.
Do you have any medical problems we should know about?
I have diabetes.
Any allergies to medications?
No known drug allergies.
Do you have any pain or any other medical complaints right now?
No, I don't have any pain or other complaints.
She has a facial droop at baseline from nerve damage after brain tumor surgery.
Pupils are about 3 millimeters. Baseline facial asymmetry is present per family report.
Right elbow skin tear is dressed. No other obvious traumatic findings are noted.
Patient is given peanut butter cups, crackers, and sweet tea to help maintain blood glucose.
Repeat blood glucose is now 119 milligrams per deciliter.
She is now answering appropriately, following commands, and appears back to her baseline mental status.
We recommend evaluation in the emergency department because low blood sugar and a fall can still have serious causes or injuries.
I understand, but I do not want to go to the hospital.
Patient is assisted off the floor to a seated position on the rollator with help from EMS and fire personnel.
We explained the limits of field assessment, including that we cannot perform imaging, lab work, or provide physician evaluation here. Patient continues to refuse transport and signs refusal.
IV is removed and the site is bandaged with gauze and tape. Bleeding is controlled.
I will call in a couple of hours to check on her.
Patient is left with facility staff after refusal, with instructions to monitor her closely and call back if symptoms return or worsen.
Hypoglycemia protocol completed with improvement after D10 and oral intake. Patient refused transport after regaining decision-making capacity.
